fact: May 7 is exactly 127 days into the year - that's like counting to 127 Mississippi's without stopping!
In the Northern Hemisphere, dandelions are typically going wild right now, shooting their fluffy seed parachutes everywhere like nature's confetti cannons.
The sun usually rises before 6 AM in most of North America by May 7, which means birds start their dawn chorus while you're probably still drooling on your pillow.
Taurus season is in full swing - people born around now are supposedly as stubborn as the bull they're named after, but also love snacks and cozy blankets.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, leaves are typically turning brilliant oranges and reds right now - it's like the trees are showing off before winter!
May 7 sits exactly 44 days after the spring equinox - nature has had just enough time to wake up and get weird.
Earthworms are usually having their busiest party season right now because the soil is finally warm and moist enough for them to wiggle around like crazy underground.
The moon can appear extra orange or pink around this time of year because spring air holds more moisture and pollen, making it look like a giant peach in the sky.
In northern regions, this is typically prime time for hearing the weirdest frog concerts ever - they're all croaking and ribbiting to find mates after their long winter naps.
Lilac bushes are usually exploding with purple flowers right now in temperate climates, and they smell so strong that one bush can make an entire neighborhood smell like fancy soap.
May 7 marks the time when daylight typically lasts about 14 hours in places like New York - that's almost enough time to watch 7 movies back-to-back!
